reason: Both answers correctly reject model quality as a durable moat and emphasize proprietary data loops and workflow embedding/switching costs. Answer A is concise but essentially a single dense sentence — it covers data loop, workflow depth, and switching costs but omits distribution/trust, reliability/evals, and doesn't address speed of iteration as the operative advantage. Answer B covers the same core points more explicitly (workflow depth with concrete mechanisms, data loops that compound vs. a non-durable model lead) and adds distribution/trust and domain-specific evaluation/reliability, which map onto the 'workflow depth, integrations, distribution' cluster the key points call for. B closes with a concrete practical move (redirect the 18 months of learning into workflow/data layers, treat the AI feature as table stakes) and a sharp closing line ('6 months ahead is worth ~zero — assume it's already gone'), giving clearer actionable guidance. B's added length is distinct, non-redundant content rather than padding, so it's more complete on the rubric while remaining scannable and organized.
